Imagine a user on a train, their internet connection flickering in and out. Most websites would fail to load, showing a frustrating “You are offline” error. But what if your website could anticipate this and still be accessible?
Not a Medium member? Read this article here
This isn’t a future technology — it’s possible today with a powerful JavaScript tool called a Service Worker. Think of a Service Worker as a programmable proxy that sits between your website and the network.1 It can intercept network requests and serve responses from a local cache, essentially making your site work offline.2
This isn’t just about convenience; it’s a hack that significantly improves user experience, page load times, and resilience. And the best part? It’s surprisingly simple to implement.
The Anatomy of the Offline Hack: Service Workers and the Cache API
At its core, this hack uses two key technologies:

• Service Worker: A script that runs in the background, separate from your main web page.3 It acts as a network proxy.4

• Cache API: A browser-native API that gives you programmatic control over a key-value store.5 This is where we’ll save our…
